content:
<USER_REQUEST>
You need to create 3 CBSE Class 12 ENGLISH CORE prediction papers for the 2027 board exam.

STEPS:
1. First, READ the latest papers to understand the exact format:
   - Read file: D:\Study Papers\Jeffrey\English Core\2026\1-3-1_Eng_lish Core.pdf
   - Read file: D:\Study Papers\Jeffrey\English Core\2025\1-1-1_English Core.pdf
   - Read file: D:\Study Papers\Jeffrey\English Core\2024\1_1_1_English Core.pdf

2. ANALYZE patterns:
   - Section structure (Reading Comprehension, Creative Writing, Literature)
   - Marks distribution per section
   - Types of reading passages
   - Writing formats (notices, invitations, letters, articles, reports)
   - Literature questions from Flamingo and Vistas
   - Internal choice patterns

3. CREATE 3 different prediction papers. Save them as:
   - D:\Study Papers\Jeffrey\English Core\Prediction Papers\English_Core_Prediction_Paper_1.md
   - D:\Study Papers\Jeffrey\English Core\Prediction Papers\English_Core_Prediction_Paper_2.md
   - D:\Study Papers\Jeffrey\English Core\Prediction Papers\English_Core_Prediction_Paper_3.md

Each paper must:
- Follow the EXACT CBSE format
- Have COMPLETE reading passages (write realistic passages of appropriate length)
- Include proper writing section questions
- Include literature questions from Flamingo: The Last Lesson, Lost Spring, Deep Water, The Rattrap, Indigo, Poets and Pancakes, The Interview, Going Places; Vistas: The Third Level, The Tiger King, Journey to the End of the Earth, The Enemy, On the Face of It, Memories of Childhood
- Include poetry questions from: My Mother at Sixty-Six, An Elementary School Classroom in a Slum, Keeping Quiet, A Thing of Beauty, Aunt Jennifer's Tigers, A Roadside Stand
- Have proper internal choices
- Include the standard CBSE header
- Be realistic for practice
</USER_REQUEST>

 ENGLISH CORE -Code No. 301  
 SAMPLE QUESTION PAPER  
CLASS -XII-(2025 -26) 
Time allowed: 3 Hrs.                                                     Maximum Marks: 80  
 
General Instructions  
Read the following instruct ions very carefully and  follow them:  
i. This que stion paper has 13 questions. All questions are compulsory.   
ii. This question paper contains three sections:  
Section A: Reading Skills,  
Section B: Creative Writing Skills

number and part thereof in your answer sheet.

v. Adhere to the prescribed word limit while answering the questions.

                                                  READING SKILLS                                               (22 marks)  
 
1. Read the following passages . 12 
Ours was the marsh country, down by the river, within twenty miles of the sea. My first most vivid 
and broad i mpression of the identity of things seems to me to have been gained on a memorable 
raw afternoon towards evening. The low leaden line beyond was the river; and that the distant 
savage lair from which the wind was rushing was the sea; and that the small bun dle of shivers 
growing afraid of it all and beginning to cry, was Pip.  
“Hold your noise!” cried a terrible voice, as a man started up from among the graves at the side of 
the bushes. “Keep still, or I’ll make you pay!”  
A fearful man, all in coarse grey, w ith a great iron on his leg. A man with no hat, and with broken 
shoes, and with an old rag tied round his head. A man who had been soaked in water, and 
smothered in mud, limped, and shivered, and glared, and growled; and whose teeth chattered in 
his head a s he seized me.  
“Tell us your name!” said the man. “Quick! Give it mouth!”  
“Pip. Pip, sir.”  
“Show us where you live,” said the man. “Point out the place!”  
The man, after looking at me for a moment, emptied my pockets. There was nothing in them but a 
piece of bread which he ate ravenously.

In India, public libraries have a rich tradition, from the ancient libraries of Nalanda and Takshashila to the modern state-run libraries found in every district today.

(2) One of the most significant roles of a library is to provide free and equal access to information. (2) Research in neuroscience suggests that writing by hand engages the brain in ways that typing does not. Long before the invention of writing, communities relied on oral narratives to pass down knowledge, cultural values, and collective memories from one generation to the next. Elders would gather around fires and share stories of heroes, gods, and the natural world, weaving lessons of morality, courage, and wisdom into their tales. In India, this tradition is deeply embedded in the social fabric, from the epics of the Ramayana and the Mahabharata to the folk tales of Panchatantra and Jataka.
